title: AP World History: Modern SAQ Practice Exam 08

questions.prompt:
Source 1: Historian Selam Tesfaye, “Christian Kingship in the Ethiopian Highlands,” modern historical interpretation, 2020.
“Solomonic Ethiopia’s rulers built authority through sacred genealogy, church patronage, monasteries, and monumental Christian culture. Political power in the highlands depended on presenting the king as defender of an ancient Christian kingdom surrounded by Muslim states and commercial rivals.”
Source 2: Historian Omar Haddad, “The Horn of Africa and Red Sea Connections,” modern historical interpretation, 2022.
“Medieval Ethiopia should not be understood only as an isolated Christian mountain kingdom. Its rulers, merchants, and religious leaders participated in Red Sea and Indian Ocean networks that linked the highlands to Egypt, Arabia, and the wider Islamic world through trade, diplomacy, and religious contact.”
(a) Identify one difference between the two historians’ interpretations of medieval Ethiopia.
(b) Describe one specific historical development that could be used to support one of the interpretations.
(c) Explain one way Ethiopian state-building during c. 1200–1450 was similar to or different from state-building in another region during the same period.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 3
(a) 1 point: Identifies a clear difference between the interpretations, such as Source 1 emphasizing Christian sacred kingship and church patronage while Source 2 emphasizes regional trade, diplomacy, and Red Sea connections.
(b) 1 point: Describes a relevant development, such as the Solomonic dynasty, Ethiopian Orthodox Christianity, rock-hewn churches, monastic networks, claims of descent from Solomon, contact with Coptic Egypt, Red Sea trade, or interactions with Muslim trading states.
(c) 1 point: Explains a valid similarity or difference with another region, such as Mali, the Delhi Sultanate, Song China, Southeast Asia, or the Swahili Coast, using specific evidence about religion, trade, environment, political legitimacy, or elite authority.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong answer should avoid treating Ethiopia as completely isolated or as only a trade society. The strongest responses recognize that Christian identity, sacred kingship, and regional connections could all contribute to political authority. For part (c), strong responses should compare a concrete method of state-building, such as religious legitimacy, control of trade, environmental adaptation, or administrative organization.

questions.prompt:
Source: Report from an Ottoman naval administrator in Algiers to officials in Istanbul, 1574. This excerpt is original but modeled on Ottoman Mediterranean administrative correspondence.
“The western sea remains unsettled. Spanish ships watch the coast, and corsair captains request powder, timber, and permission to recruit sailors from many towns. If the harbor is repaired and the fortress supplied, the sultan’s name will be honored from Tunis to the islands. Local notables must be treated with care, for they know the tribes and markets better than men sent from the capital. Yet without imperial guns and ships, loyalty here will be no stronger than the next fleet that appears offshore.”
(a) Identify the likely purpose of the report.
(b) Explain how the report reflects one challenge faced by maritime or gunpowder empires during c. 1450–1750.
(c) Explain one limitation of the source as evidence for the views of people living under Ottoman authority in North Africa.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 3
(a) 1 point: Identifies a plausible purpose, such as requesting supplies, urging harbor repairs, strengthening Ottoman naval power, warning about Spanish rivalry, or advising cooperation with local elites.
(b) 1 point: Explains a relevant challenge, such as maintaining distant coastal provinces, competing with European naval powers, supplying fortresses, integrating local elites, managing corsairs, controlling trade routes, or projecting authority across maritime spaces.
(c) 1 point: Explains a valid limitation, such as the source representing an Ottoman administrative and military perspective rather than merchants, sailors, enslaved captives, rural communities, women, religious minorities, or local opponents; or noting that it emphasizes imperial security while minimizing local grievances.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong response should use the author’s position as a naval administrator. The report is concerned with imperial security, local cooperation, and Mediterranean rivalry. Strong answers should connect the Ottoman example to broader early modern patterns of maritime competition without assuming that all North African communities experienced Ottoman rule in the same way.

questions.prompt:
Source: Description of a Soviet wartime factory poster, 1942.
The poster shows women operating metal lathes in a large factory while tanks roll out of the building in the background. A map on the wall marks railway lines leading east from cities labeled “Leningrad,” “Moscow,” and “Kharkiv” toward industrial centers beyond the Ural Mountains. A slogan reads: “Every Shift at the Machine Is a Blow against the Invader.” At the bottom, smaller text calls for “discipline, production, and sacrifice for victory.”
(a) Identify one wartime development suggested by the described poster.
(b) Explain one way total war affected societies during World War II.
(c) Explain one way the poster reflects the relationship between government propaganda and wartime mobilization.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 3
(a) 1 point: Identifies a valid wartime development, such as women’s industrial labor, factory mobilization, military production, relocation of industry, railway movement of resources, or civilian participation in war production.
(b) 1 point: Explains a valid social effect of total war, such as expanded state control over labor, rationing, women’s participation in industry, civilian sacrifice, bombing and displacement, propaganda campaigns, mass conscription, or reorganization of economies for military needs.
(c) 1 point: Explains how propaganda encouraged production, discipline, sacrifice, national unity, gendered participation, or support for the war effort by linking factory labor directly to military victory.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong response should interpret the poster as a tool of wartime mobilization, not as neutral evidence of factory life. The women workers, tanks, railway map, and slogan all connect civilian labor to military survival. Strong answers should explain how total war blurred the line between battlefield and home front.
